title: Verification Queue
text: This page tracks which dataset entries still need manual verification against source repository records and primary publications. The queue is conservative: entries remain here unless they have been reviewed and promoted to a higher verification status. Presence in the queue does not mean an entry is wrong; it means the metadata should not yet be treated as final.

title: Verification Queue
text: Queue Summary

title: Verification Queue
text:
Verification Status Definitions







Status
Meaning




pending_review
Initial listing; not fully verified


source_checked
Source record opens and matches the accession or resource


metadata_checked
Disease group, sample type, platform, and access metadata checked against source


publication_checked
Primary publication title, year, DOI, and PMID checked where available


analysis_ready_candidate
Candidate for downstream analysis after source, metadata, publication, and access checks; still not a guarantee of fitness for every use case


needs_revision
Source evidence conflicts with current registry metadata or key fields are obsolete

title: Verification Queue
text:
Manual Review Rule
Rows should remain in the queue when any of the following are true:

source URL is missing or does not point directly to the intended record
publication title, year, DOI, or PMID is unclear
disease groups, sample type, platform, or access status are unclear
the resource is a broad portal or multi-accession aggregate
individual-level data require controlled access
source evidence contradicts the current row

Do not promote a row to analysis_ready_candidate without a human review of the evidence and the intended analysis context.

title: Repositories and Portals
text: IBD omics datasets are distributed across numerous public repositories, specialized portals, and consortium-specific platforms. This page describes the major repositories represented in the dataset registry, their access models, and relevant caveats. Inclusion here reflects the presence of IBD-relevant data, not an endorsement or ranking of any resource.

title: Repositories and Portals
text:
Genomics and Transcriptomics Archives

GEO — Gene Expression Omnibus



Repository type
Public archive for functional genomics data


Data types hosted
Microarray, RNA-seq, scRNA-seq, methylation arrays, ChIP-seq, ATAC-seq, and related high-throughput data


Access model
Publicly accessible; some datasets have controlled-access raw data (SRA)


IBD data
Largest collection of IBD transcriptomic and epigenomic datasets; many accessions GSExxxxxx


URL
ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo



Notes. GEO hosts the majority of bulk and single-cell transcriptomic IBD datasets. Processed data (expression matrices, metadata) are typically freely accessible. Raw sequencing reads may be deposited in SRA and may require additional steps to access. Metadata completeness and clinical annotation quality vary substantially between submissions.



SRA — Sequence Read Archive



Repository type
Public archive for raw sequencing data


Data types hosted
Raw FASTQ and BAM files from next-generation sequencing experiments


Access model
Publicly accessible (open access) or controlled access (dbGaP-linked studies)


IBD data
Raw sequencing data from RNA-seq, scRNA-seq, metagenomics, metatranscriptomics, and related IBD studies


URL
ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/sra



Notes. Raw data downloads from SRA can be very large. Access to controlled-access SRA studies requires dbGaP authorization. The SRA toolkit (prefetch, fastq-dump) is typically required for downloads.



ArrayExpress / BioStudies (EMBL-EBI)



Repository type
Public archive for functional genomics and multi-omics data


Data types hosted
Microarray, RNA-seq, and other functional genomics data; expanding to multi-omics


Access model
Publicly accessible


IBD data
European counterpart to GEO; some IBD datasets deposited here rather than or in addition to GEO


URL
ebi.ac.uk/biostudies



Notes. Many European IBD studies deposit data in ArrayExpress/BioStudies rather than GEO. Some datasets are mirrored between both archives.

title: Repositories and Portals
text:
Single-Cell and Spatial Portals

CELLxGENE (CZI Science)



Repository type
Public portal for single-cell and spatial transcriptomics data


Data types hosted
scRNA-seq, spatial transcriptomics; standardized with Cell Ontology annotations


Access model
Publicly accessible; data downloadable in H5AD format


IBD data
Multiple IBD scRNA-seq datasets with curated cell annotations


URL
cellxgene.cziscience.com



Notes. CELLxGENE enforces a standardized metadata schema including cell ontology terms, tissue annotations, disease terms (using MONDO), and organism. This makes cross-study comparison more tractable than raw GEO deposits. Interactive exploration is available via the browser, and data can be downloaded for offline analysis.



Human Cell Atlas (HCA)



Repository type
International consortium portal for single-cell reference atlases


Data types hosted
scRNA-seq, spatial transcriptomics, multi-omics; reference atlas datasets


Access model
Publicly accessible; some controlled-access components


IBD data
IBD-related intestinal atlas datasets; normal gut cell type references


URL
humancellatlas.org



Notes. HCA datasets can also be accessed via CELLxGENE. The HCA Data Portal provides dataset-level metadata and download links. Some HCA datasets require registration; others are fully open.

title: Repositories and Portals
text:
Multi-Omics Portals

IBDMDB — Inflammatory Bowel Disease Multi-omics Database



Repository type
IBD-specific multi-omics data portal (iHMP project)


Data types hosted
Host transcriptomics, metagenomics, metatranscriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, virome; longitudinal stool and biopsy data


Access model
Processed data publicly accessible via portal; some raw data components via HMP and dbGaP


IBD data
Core HMP2 longitudinal IBD cohort (CD, UC, non-IBD controls)


URL
ibdmdb.org



Notes. IBDMDB is one of the most comprehensive public IBD multi-omics resources. The portal allows interactive browsing and download of processed data. Some raw data require dbGaP authorization. Longitudinal design (multiple timepoints per participant) adds analytical complexity but also unique value for dynamic analyses.

title: Repositories and Portals
text:
Proteomics Archives

PRIDE / ProteomeXchange



Repository type
Public archive for proteomics mass spectrometry data


Data types hosted
Raw MS files (mzML, RAW), processed results (mzIdentML, mzTab), peptide/protein quantification tables


Access model
Publicly accessible


IBD data
Fecal proteomics, mucosal proteomics, metaproteomics datasets


URL
ebi.ac.uk/pride



Notes. PRIDE is part of the ProteomeXchange consortium. Accessions are prefixed PXD. Raw MS files are typically large. Re-analysis requires appropriate proteomics software (MaxQuant, Proteome Discoverer, Spectronaut, etc.) and appropriate reference databases.



MassIVE



Repository type
Public archive for mass spectrometry data (UCSD)


Data types hosted
Proteomics and metabolomics MS data; cross-referenced with ProteomeXchange


Access model
Publicly accessible


IBD data
Fecal proteomics and metabolomics IBD datasets


URL
massive.ucsd.edu



Notes. MassIVE accessions are prefixed MSV. Several IBD fecal proteomics/metabolomics datasets are deposited here. Can be cross-referenced with PRIDE via ProteomeXchange identifiers.

title: Repositories and Portals
text:
Metabolomics

Metabolomics Workbench (NIH)



Repository type
Public repository for metabolomics data (NIH Common Fund)


Data types hosted
Metabolomics and lipidomics raw and processed data; experimental metadata


Access model
Publicly accessible


IBD data
IBD metabolomics studies; stool and serum metabolomics


URL
metabolomicsworkbench.org



Notes. Accessions are prefixed ST (study) and DS (dataset). The portal includes experimental metadata, sample information, and download options for processed data tables. Raw spectral files are often available for re-analysis.

title: Repositories and Portals
text:
Controlled-Access Archives

dbGaP — Database of Genotypes and Phenotypes



Repository type
Controlled-access archive for human genotype and phenotype data (NIH)


Data types hosted
Individual-level genotype data, GWAS, WGS, clinical phenotypes


Access model
Controlled access; requires institutional data access request and approval


IBD data
IIBDGC individual-level GWAS data; some IBD multi-omics studies with controlled-access components


URL
ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gap



Notes. Access requires submission of a Data Access Request (DAR) through the dbGaP portal, institutional sign-off, and approval by the data access committee. Processing times vary. Approved data must be accessed in a secure computing environment consistent with the data use agreement.

title: Repositories and Portals
text:
Genetics Portals and Resources

GWAS Catalog (EMBL-EBI)



Repository type
Curated catalog of published GWAS results and summary statistics


Data types hosted
GWAS summary statistics, variant-trait associations, study metadata


Access model
Publicly accessible


IBD data
IBD, CD, and UC GWAS results from numerous published studies


URL
ebi.ac.uk/gwas



Notes. The GWAS Catalog aggregates published GWAS results with standardized trait ontology annotations (EFO terms). Summary statistics files are available for many studies. Useful for locus lookup, phenotype comparison, and identifying published IBD GWAS signals.



IIBDGC — International IBD Genetics Consortium



Repository type
IBD genetics consortium; summary statistics and controlled-access data


Data types hosted
GWAS summary statistics, fine-mapping results, individual-level data (via dbGaP)


Access model
Summary statistics publicly accessible; individual-level data controlled-access via dbGaP


IBD data
Large-scale IBD, CD, and UC GWAS meta-analyses


URL
ibdgenetics.org



Notes. IIBDGC has produced some of the largest IBD GWAS to date. Summary statistics from published analyses are often available via the IIBDGC website or the GWAS Catalog. Individual participant data require dbGaP authorization.



Open Targets Genetics / Platform



Repository type
Integrated genomics and drug target platform


Data types hosted
Integrated GWAS, QTL, fine-mapping, and target evidence; not a primary data archive


Access model
Publicly accessible


IBD data
IBD/CD/UC target evidence integrated from GWAS and functional genomics


URL
genetics.opentargets.org



Notes. Open Targets is not a primary data archive but an integrative platform derived from public GWAS and functional genomics data. Useful for target prioritization and locus-to-gene mapping. The underlying data sources should be verified for any specific analysis.



FinnGen and UK Biobank



Repository type
Population biobank GWAS resources


Data types hosted
GWAS summary statistics (public); individual-level data (controlled access)


Access model
Summary statistics publicly accessible (FinnGen results browser, Open GWAS/MRC IEU); individual data require access application


IBD data
IBD, CD, and UC phenotypes available in both biobanks


URL
FinnGen: finngen.fi  



Notes. FinnGen provides GWAS summary statistics publicly via its results browser. UK Biobank individual-level data require application through the UK Biobank Access Management System. IBD phenotype definitions in biobanks rely on ICD codes and may differ from clinically adjudicated cohorts.

Repository details reflect publicly available information at the time of curation. Access policies, URLs, and available data may change. Always consult the repository directly for current access requirements.

title: Omics Layers
text: This page organizes the dataset inventory by omics layer. Each section describes the layer, typical specimen types, common analytical caveats, and the datasets currently indexed for that layer. Counts are derived dynamically from the dataset registry.

title: Omics Layers
text:
Bulk Transcriptomics


Specimens: intestinal biopsies, blood, peripheral blood mononuclear cells  |  Platforms: microarray (Affymetrix, Illumina), RNA-seq

Count in registry: 16 datasets
Bulk transcriptomic datasets measure the average gene expression across all cells in a tissue or biological sample. In IBD research, these datasets typically derive from colonic or ileal mucosal biopsies, peripheral blood, or whole blood. They have been widely used to identify gene expression differences between disease states (active vs. remission, CD vs. UC, disease vs. control) and to discover transcriptomic biomarkers.
Typical uses. Mucosal or blood gene expression profiling; disease-vs-control comparison; CD-vs-UC-vs-control comparisons; biomarker discovery; pathway analysis; deconvolution input.
Common caveats. Bulk measurements average across heterogeneous cell populations, masking cell-type-specific signals. Cross-study comparisons require attention to tissue site (ileum vs. colon vs. blood), disease activity, treatment status at biopsy, platform (microarray vs. RNA-seq), batch effects, and normalization. Age groups (pediatric vs. adult) differ in biology and should not be pooled without justification. Many older microarray datasets lack detailed clinical metadata.

title: Omics Layers
text:
Single-Cell Transcriptomics


Specimens: intestinal biopsies (dissociated), lamina propria isolates  |  Platforms: 10x Genomics Chromium, other droplet-based scRNA-seq

Count in registry: 5 datasets
Single-cell RNA sequencing resolves gene expression at the level of individual cells, enabling cell-type and cell-state characterization. In IBD, scRNA-seq has been applied to characterize immune, epithelial, stromal, and endothelial cell populations in the intestinal mucosa, enabling discovery of disease-associated cell states and pathways.
Typical uses. Cell-type and cell-state atlases; identification of disease-associated cell states; deconvolution reference panels; single-cell disease-vs-control comparisons; treatment response at cellular resolution.
Common caveats. scRNA-seq datasets are large and computationally demanding. Cell annotation requires reference databases and expert review. Batch effects arise from sample processing, dissociation protocols, and sequencing runs. Cross-study integration requires harmonization of cell annotations and normalization strategies. Dropout (zero-inflation) and ambient RNA are dataset-specific issues. Sample sizes per group are often smaller than bulk studies.

title: Omics Layers
text:
Spatial Transcriptomics


Specimens: formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) or fresh-frozen tissue sections  |  Platforms: 10x Visium, CosMx SMI, Xenium, Slide-seq

Count in registry: 1 dataset
Spatial transcriptomics maps gene expression while preserving the spatial location of cells within tissue sections. This enables characterization of cellular neighborhoods, tissue architectural changes in inflammation, and spatially restricted gene expression patterns not detectable in dissociated single-cell or bulk assays.
Typical uses. Spatial mapping of inflammatory cell states; tissue architecture analysis; niche and co-localization analysis; spatial characterization of ulcerated vs. inflamed vs. uninflamed regions.
Common caveats. Resolution and sensitivity vary substantially across platforms. Spot-based platforms (Visium) capture multiple cells per spot; single-cell resolution platforms (CosMx, Xenium) measure a limited gene panel. Data volumes are large. Normalization and cell-type deconvolution are non-trivial. Cross-study comparisons are difficult due to platform heterogeneity and panel differences.

title: Omics Layers
text:
Microbiome / Metagenomics / Metatranscriptomics


Specimens: stool, mucosal biopsies  |  Platforms: shotgun metagenomics, 16S rRNA, metatranscriptomics, viromics

Count in registry: 1 dataset
Gut microbiome datasets characterize the microbial community composition and functional activity in stool or mucosal specimens. These datasets include shotgun metagenomics (microbial DNA sequencing), metatranscriptomics (microbial RNA expression), and virome profiling. The gut microbiome is substantially altered in IBD, and multi-omics studies combine microbial and host data.
Typical uses. Microbial community profiling; host–microbiome integration; functional pathway analysis; longitudinal microbiome dynamics; stool biomarker discovery.
Common caveats. Substantial inter-individual variability. Stool microbiome is affected by diet, antibiotics, and sample handling. Reference databases for annotation are incomplete. Species- and strain-level resolution requires deep sequencing. Cross-study comparisons are complicated by extraction protocols, sequencing depth, and bioinformatic pipelines.

title: Omics Layers
text:
Proteomics / Metaproteomics


Specimens: colon biopsies, stool  |  Platforms: LC-MS/MS, DIA-MS, shotgun proteomics

Count in registry: 5 datasets
Proteomic datasets measure protein abundance in biological samples. In IBD, proteomics has been applied to intestinal mucosal tissue and stool (fecal proteomics). Fecal proteomics and metaproteomics capture both host-derived and microbial proteins and offer non-invasive sampling. Mucosal proteomics complements transcriptomic data by capturing post-translational changes.
Typical uses. Mucosal protein biomarker discovery; non-invasive fecal protein biomarkers; host–microbiome protein profiling; proteomics–transcriptomics integration.
Common caveats. Fecal proteomics is susceptible to dietary variation, microbial variability, and sample processing differences. Mucosal proteomics is limited by tissue availability. Database coverage for microbial proteins (metaproteomics) remains incomplete. Quantitative methods (DDA vs. DIA) differ in sensitivity and reproducibility.

title: Omics Layers
text:
Metabolomics / Lipidomics


Specimens: stool, serum, plasma, urine, tissue  |  Platforms: LC-MS, GC-MS, NMR

Count in registry: 3 datasets
Metabolomic datasets measure small-molecule metabolites in biological samples. In IBD, metabolomics has been applied to stool, serum, and tissue, capturing changes in host and microbial metabolic activity. Lipidomics focuses specifically on lipid species. These datasets are often combined with microbiome or proteomics data in multi-omics studies.
Typical uses. Metabolic biomarker discovery; disease severity correlates; treatment response metabolomics; microbiome–metabolome integration.
Common caveats. Metabolomics is sensitive to sample handling, storage, and analytical variation. Platform differences (LC-MS, GC-MS, NMR) affect coverage and quantitation. Annotation of spectral features to specific metabolites remains incomplete. Fecal metabolomics reflects both host and microbial metabolism.

title: Omics Layers
text:
Methylation / Epigenomics


Specimens: peripheral blood, colon mucosa, intestinal epithelial cells  |  Platforms: Illumina 450K/EPIC methylation arrays, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq

Count in registry: 5 datasets
Methylation and epigenomic datasets characterize DNA methylation or chromatin accessibility patterns in IBD-relevant tissues. Blood methylation datasets have been used for EWAS (epigenome-wide association studies). Mucosal methylation datasets link epigenetic changes to the inflammatory environment of the intestine.
Typical uses. Epigenetic biomarker discovery; EWAS; mucosal epigenetic changes in IBD; blood methylation CD vs. UC comparisons; epigenetic–transcriptomic integration.
Common caveats. Blood methylation is substantially confounded by cell-type composition; cell-type deconvolution (e.g., Houseman method) is necessary. Methylation arrays have fixed probe sets and cannot capture all genomic regions. Mucosal methylation is influenced by inflammatory cell infiltration. Older 450K datasets have lower genomic coverage than EPIC arrays.

title: Omics Layers
text:
Genetics / GWAS


Specimens: DNA from blood or saliva  |  Platforms: SNP arrays (Illumina, Affymetrix), imputed genotypes, whole-genome sequencing

Count in registry: 5 datasets
Genetic and GWAS datasets provide genome-wide association study summary statistics or individual-level genotype data for IBD, Crohn’s disease, and ulcerative colitis. These resources underpin genetic risk factor identification, fine-mapping, target prioritization, Mendelian randomization, and polygenic risk score development.
Typical uses. Genetic risk factor identification; target prioritization; Mendelian randomization; colocalization with QTL data; polygenic risk scores; trans-ancestral analyses.
Common caveats. Individual-level genotype data are typically controlled-access. Summary statistics are often publicly available but differ in imputation panels, ancestry composition, and case/control definitions. IBD phenotype ascertainment varies across studies (clinical diagnosis vs. ICD codes). Multi-ancestry analyses require specialized methods.

title: Omics Layers
text:
Multi-Omics


Specimens: multiple per subject  |  Platforms: multiple integrated platforms

Count in registry: 1 dataset
Multi-omics datasets integrate two or more omics layers collected from the same subjects or same biobank. These resources support integrative analyses linking, for example, host transcriptomics with microbiome, proteomics, and metabolomics data.
Typical uses. Host–microbiome integration; cross-omics correlation; multi-omics biomarker discovery; longitudinal multi-omics dynamics.
Common caveats. Not all samples in a multi-omics study have complete data across all layers. Data integration requires careful harmonization of metadata, sample identifiers, and normalization. Batch effects may differ across omics layers. Access conditions may vary by omics component.





All counts and tables on this page are derived from data/ibd_omics_datasets.csv and update automatically as the registry expands. Many entries carry Needs verification status — metadata should be confirmed against source repositories before use in analysis.

title: IBD Omics Explorer
text:
Dataset Registry



A curated, searchable inventory of publicly available omics datasets from inflammatory bowel disease research — across bulk transcriptomics, single-cell transcriptomics, spatial transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, methylation, microbiome, and genetics.


Browse All Datasets Integrated Resources Explore by Omics Layer Browse by Use Case Verification Queue Orchestrator Curation Methods
Primary dataset counts are derived from the current dataset CSV. Integrated resources are tracked separately because they combine, curate, or reprocess multiple source datasets.

title: IBD Omics Explorer
text:
A curated, searchable inventory of publicly available omics datasets from inflammatory bowel disease research — across bulk transcriptomics, single-cell transcriptomics, spatial transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, methylation, microbiome, and genetics.


Browse All Datasets Integrated Resources Explore by Omics Layer Browse by Use Case Verification Queue Orchestrator Curation Methods

title: IBD Omics Explorer
text:
Scope
This explorer aims to map publicly indexed IBD omics datasets across all major omics layers. The focus is on datasets derived from human subjects with inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn’s disease, ulcerative colitis, or IBD-unclassified), or from non-IBD controls used in IBD research contexts. Datasets may include tissue biopsies, blood, stool, organoids, or other relevant specimen types.
Inclusion does not imply that a dataset is fully reusable, high quality, or suitable for any particular analysis. The registry documents what is publicly accessible, along with known caveats and limitations.
See the Curation Methods page for detailed inclusion criteria, data access definitions, and metadata caveats.

title: IBD Omics Explorer
text:
Data Layers Covered

 🧬

Bulk Transcriptomics


Tissue- and blood-level gene expression from microarray and RNA-seq platforms

  🔬

Single-Cell Transcriptomics


Cell-type and cell-state resolution expression in IBD tissues

  🗺️

Spatial Transcriptomics


Spatially resolved gene expression preserving tissue architecture

  🦠

Microbiome / Metagenomics


Gut microbial community profiling via shotgun metagenomics and metatranscriptomics

  🧪

Proteomics / Metaproteomics


Mucosal and fecal proteomics, including host and microbial proteins

  ⚗️

Metabolomics / Lipidomics


Small-molecule profiling in stool, blood, and tissue

  🔖

Methylation / Epigenomics


DNA methylation arrays and chromatin accessibility in blood and mucosa

  🧩

Genetics / GWAS


IBD GWAS summary statistics and individual-level genotype resources

title: IBD Omics Explorer
text:
Navigating the Registry



Page
Purpose




Datasets
Searchable and filterable table of all indexed datasets


Omics Layers
Inventory grouped by omics type with layer-level notes


Repositories
Overview of archives and portals hosting IBD omics data


Use Cases
Browse datasets tagged as potentially relevant to specific research questions


Verification Queue
Review source-check status, citation evidence, and metadata gaps


Orchestrator
Track active improvement tasks, missing-resource audits, and next curation priorities


Curation Methods
Inclusion criteria, access definitions, metadata caveats


About
Project context and contact information





Disclaimer. This website is a curated research tool. It is intended to support dataset discovery, not to serve as a complete or authoritative registry. Dataset availability, metadata accuracy, and access status should be verified independently before analysis. This site does not rank datasets or endorse any particular resource over another.

title: Curation Methods
text: This page documents the curation rules used to build and maintain the IBD Omics Explorer dataset registry. Understanding these rules is essential for interpreting the annotations and deciding whether a listed dataset is appropriate for a specific analysis.

title: Curation Methods
text:
Purpose and Scope
The IBD Omics Explorer is designed to map and organize publicly available IBD omics datasets across all major omics layers, with the goal of supporting dataset discovery. It is not a systematic review, meta-analysis, or quality-assessment resource. Inclusion in this registry does not imply that a dataset is high quality, fully reusable, or suitable for any particular analysis.
The registry aims to be inclusive and expandable. The current dataset is a starting point, not a comprehensive catalog. Many datasets that qualify for inclusion may not yet be listed.

title: Curation Methods
text:
Current Registry State
The current dataset registry contains 113 primary dataset entries plus 10 integrated resources tracked separately. The newer systematic-discovery rows are intentionally marked as source_checked rather than fully verified until their publication links, sample counts, and cohort labels are reviewed row by row. This means the website is most reliable as a discovery map and triage tool, not as a final source of study-level metadata.
Before using an entry for analysis, verify the source repository record directly, including disease groups, sample counts, specimen type, assay platform, access conditions, and publication links. Entries with Unclear fields should be treated as leads for manual curation rather than confirmed facts.

title: Curation Methods
text:
Retired Primary Dataset IDs
Some DSxxx identifiers are intentionally retired when a broad portal, consortium, or integrated resource is moved out of the primary accession-level dataset table. Retired identifiers are not reused. This keeps older references interpretable while preventing resource-level rows from being counted as single primary datasets.
Retired identifiers are tracked in data/retired_dataset_ids.csv.

title: Curation Methods
text:
Priority Verification Queue
The following entry types should be checked first because they carry the highest risk of misleading users:








Priority
Entry pattern
Why it matters




1
Entries with dataset_url = Unclear
Users cannot verify the source record directly from the site


2
Entries with publication_title = Unclear and publication_year = Unclear
Study provenance is incomplete


3
Aggregated portals or multi-accession resources
These can mix studies with different assays, cohorts, and access rules


4
Entries where sample type, platform, or disease groups are partly inferred
These fields determine whether datasets can be pooled or compared


5
Controlled-access genetics or multi-omics resources
Public indexing does not mean the underlying individual-level files are downloadable



Concrete current examples include GSE accessions with unknown publication metadata and treatment-trial rows where sample counts or publication linkage still need row-level checking. Integrated databases, atlases, and broad genetics/platform entries are tracked separately on the Integrated Resources page.

title: Curation Methods
text:
Inclusion Criteria
A dataset is eligible for inclusion if it meets at least one of the following criteria:

Human subjects with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), Crohn’s disease (CD), ulcerative colitis (UC), or IBD-unclassified (IBD-U)
Non-IBD control samples collected in the context of an IBD study
Human intestinal, colonic, ileal, blood, stool, organoid, or microbiome samples analyzed with omics methods in an IBD research context
A publicly registered accession, portal entry, or publicly accessible summary statistics
A curated database specifically aggregating IBD omics studies (e.g., IBDTransDB)
A controlled-access resource that is publicly indexed (e.g., dbGaP) and thus searchable and describable

title: Curation Methods
text:
Exclusion or Deferred Categories
The following categories are excluded or deferred pending additional review:







Category
Handling




Animal-only datasets (mouse, rat models)
Excluded unless they directly describe human companion datasets


Purely mechanistic cell-line datasets
Excluded unless they include primary patient-derived material


Datasets without any accessible metadata
Deferred until metadata can be located


Duplicate submissions
Retained only if they add useful metadata, processed files, or additional accession cross-links


Publication-only datasets
Included with Publication only or Unclear access status and a note


Unpublished preprint-only datasets
May be included with explicit annotation; treat with caution

title: Curation Methods
text:
Public Data Access Labels
The registry uses the following standardized labels for the data_access_status field:







Label
Meaning




Public raw data
Raw data files (FASTQ, CEL, IDAT, mzML, mzIdentML, mzTab, count matrices, BAM, or equivalent) are publicly downloadable without application


Public processed data
Processed expression matrix, abundance table, or summary statistics are publicly available, but raw data may not be


Public portal
Data are accessible and explorable through a public portal or database interface, with or without direct file download


Controlled access
Dataset is publicly indexed and described, but access to data files requires an application, institutional approval, or data use agreement


Publication only
Dataset is described in a publication, but reusable data files are not clearly available in a repository


Unclear
A repository record exists but data availability, metadata, or access conditions could not be confidently determined from public information



Multiple labels may apply to a single dataset (e.g., Public processed data; Controlled access (some components)).

title: Curation Methods
text:
Curation Status Labels
Each entry carries a curation_status label indicating the degree of metadata verification:







Label
Meaning




Needs verification
Metadata or access status should be manually checked before relying on the annotation


Partially verified
One or more source, metadata, access, or citation checks have been completed, but the row is not analysis-ready


Verified
Metadata and access links have been manually checked against the source repository record


Deprecated/limited
Dataset exists but has limited reuse value, incomplete public access, or substantial data quality issues



Most entries currently require additional verification. Users are encouraged to verify directly against source repositories before initiating analysis.

title: Curation Methods
text:
Metadata Field Definitions







Field
Definition




dataset_id
Internal registry identifier (DSxxx)


dataset_name
Human-readable dataset name or accession-based label


accession
Primary accession number(s) at the source repository


repository
Repository or portal where data are hosted


repository_type
Type of repository (public archive, portal, consortium, etc.)


publication_title
Title of the primary associated publication (if known)


publication_year
Year of primary publication (if known)


doi
DOI of the primary publication (if known)


pmid
PubMed identifier for the primary publication (if verified)


disease
Disease groups included in the dataset


population
Age group or population descriptor (Adult, Pediatric, Mixed, Unclear)


sample_type
Type of biological specimen (biopsy, blood, stool, etc.)


tissue_site
Anatomical site of the specimen


omics_layer
Omics data type(s) included


platform
Assay platform(s) used


study_design
Study design descriptor


sample_size_subjects
Approximate number of subjects (may refer to participants, samples, or sequencing libraries depending on study)


sample_size_samples
Approximate number of samples (may differ from subjects in paired or longitudinal designs)


raw_data_available
Whether raw data files are publicly accessible (Yes / No / Partial / Unclear)


processed_data_available
Whether processed data files are publicly accessible


controlled_access
Whether any component requires controlled access


clinical_metadata
Whether clinical metadata (diagnosis, disease activity, demographics) are available


endoscopy_available
Whether endoscopy data are available alongside omics data


treatment_metadata
Whether treatment exposure information is available


longitudinal
Whether the dataset includes longitudinal follow-up


potential_use_cases
Metadata tags describing possible research applications


limitations
Known limitations of the dataset relevant to reuse


data_access_status
Standardized access label (see above)


curation_status
Curation status label (see above)


dataset_url
URL to the dataset record (if known)


publication_url
URL to the primary publication (if known)


notes
Free-text curation notes


study_family
Conservative cohort, trial, or source-family label used to identify rows that may come from the same study population, clinical trial, or reused source cohort


component_of_dataset_id
Parent dataset identifier when a row is explicitly retained as a component of another primary dataset row; blank when no parent row is assigned


count_as_independent_cohort
Conservative overcounting flag indicating whether the row can currently be counted as an independent cohort (TRUE, FALSE, or Unclear)


overlap_notes
Short explanation of known or suspected cohort overlap and how the row should be counted


verification_status
Current evidence-check status for the row


source_record_checked
Whether the source record has been manually checked


publication_checked
Whether the publication citation has been manually checked


data_access_checked
Whether raw/processed/controlled access status has been manually checked


sample_metadata_checked
Whether disease groups, sample type, platform, and sample counts have been manually checked


verification_date
Date of most recent verification check


verified_by
Person or workflow that performed the verification check


verification_notes
Evidence-backed notes on what was checked and what remains uncertain


evidence_url
Source URLs used to support the current verification status


citation_verified
Whether DOI/PMID/publication metadata have been checked


usable_for_*
Conservative use-case flags used by the Use Cases page; values are TRUE, FALSE, or Unclear

title: Curation Methods
text:
Verification Workflow
The verification workflow is deliberately conservative:

New rows start as pending_review.
A row moves to source_checked only after the source URL opens and matches the intended accession or resource.
A row moves to metadata_checked only after key study metadata are checked against a source record.
A row moves to publication_checked only after citation fields are checked against a publication, DOI, PubMed, or repository-linked citation.
A row becomes needs_revision if source evidence contradicts the current registry row.
A row should only become analysis_ready_candidate after human review of source, metadata, publication, access status, and the intended analysis context.

Verification batches cover selected high-value/high-risk entries across multi-omics, single-cell, proteomics, metabolomics, methylation, transcriptomics, and controlled-access genetics resources. Atlases, portals, curated databases, and consortium resources are tracked on the Integrated Resources page because they are not single accession-level datasets. Systematic-discovery additions are retained as source_checked until they receive deeper row-level verification.

title: Curation Methods
text:
Cohort Families and Overlap
Some registry rows describe different accessions, assays, timepoints, or derived resources from the same underlying study population. These rows are useful as separate dataset records, but their subject or sample counts should not automatically be summed.
When available, cohort-family review fields identify related rows, paired modality derivatives, and rows that should be treated as components of a shared study family rather than independent cohorts. Rows marked as uncertain should be interpreted conservatively until source-level sample IDs or study documentation are reviewed.
For de-duplicated cohort counts, use the cohort-family audit files rather than summing the main dataset table directly.
Therapeutic-family reviews are included when available, but rows requiring source-level sample-ID review should be treated as provisional for cohort counting.
The primary dataset CSV now includes appended cohort-family fields (study_family, component_of_dataset_id, count_as_independent_cohort, and overlap_notes). These fields are intentionally conservative and currently appear in the Dataset Explorer detail view only.
All 113 primary dataset rows now have a study_family label, representing 92 distinct study or source families. count_as_independent_cohort = FALSE means the row should not be counted as a separate cohort because it is a known component, subset, or overlapping accession for another row. Unclear means the row is related to another study family, but public metadata are not sufficient to reconcile donors or samples confidently. Users should treat Unclear rows conservatively and avoid summing them without source-level review.
Users can search by study, cohort, or trial names such as PROTECT, RISK, IBD200, ACT1, UNITI, UNIFI, GEMINI, BERGAMOT, scIBD, or SP140. These labels are intended for discoverability and cohort accounting; they do not replace direct verification of the source repository before analysis. Current countability values are 91 TRUE, 9 FALSE, and 13 Unclear.
Detailed review artifacts:

Cohort overlap audit
PROTECT overlap review
RISK pediatric CD overlap review
IBD200 overlap review
Therapeutic overlap review, provisional
Unclear countability review pack
Study-family completeness QA
Cohort-counting QA report
Remaining unclear consistency audit
PROTECT methylation crosswalk
RISK countability policy review
SP140 donor crosswalk
DS035 GWAS countability policy review
DS061/DS088 infliximab crosswalk

title: Curation Methods
text:
Metadata Caveats
Users of this registry should be aware of the following general caveats:
Sample size. Sample size fields may refer to subjects, unique samples, biopsies, cells, sequencing libraries, or files, depending on the study. These are not always interchangeable. Actual analyzable sample sizes per disease group should be verified from the publication or repository metadata.
Disease definitions. IBD, CD, UC, and IBD-U definitions vary across studies. Some studies use clinician-assigned diagnoses; others use ICD codes or self-report. Disease activity definitions (active vs. remission) and scoring systems differ. Cross-study comparisons of disease groups require attention to diagnostic criteria.
Treatment exposure. Treatment status at the time of sample collection is often incompletely annotated in public deposits. Even where annotated, the specific agents, dosing, duration, and response status may not be consistently available.
Pediatric vs. adult. Age group classification may not always be explicitly labeled. Unlabeled entries should not be assumed to be adult. Biological differences between pediatric and adult IBD are well established; pooling across age groups is generally not recommended without justification.
Tissue site. Tissue site annotation may be incomplete, inconsistent across studies, or ambiguous (e.g., “colon” without specifying anatomical segment). Cross-study comparisons should account for tissue site differences.
Platform effects. Different omics platforms (microarray vs. RNA-seq; different sequencing depths; different array generations) introduce systematic differences that must be accounted for in cross-study analyses.
Batch effects. Omics data are subject to batch effects from sample processing, sequencing run, laboratory, and reagent lot variation. Batch correction is typically required for cross-study comparisons.
Controlled-access data. Controlled-access resources are described for completeness but require institutional approval before any data can be downloaded or analyzed. Approval processes vary and can take weeks to months.
Unclear fields. Fields marked “Unclear” indicate that the information could not be confidently determined from publicly available records at the time of curation. These should be verified before use.

title: Curation Methods
text:
How to Contribute or Correct
This registry is designed to expand continuously. If you identify:

A dataset that should be included
A metadata error or outdated access status
A deprecated or moved accession
A verified entry that should be updated from Needs verification to Verified

Please open an issue or pull request on the source repository.

title: Curation Methods
text:
Data Sources for Curation
Metadata in this registry should be checked against:

Public repository records, including GEO, SRA, PRIDE, MassIVE, Metabolomics Workbench, dbGaP, GWAS Catalog, CELLxGENE, and Human Cell Atlas
Consortium and project portals, including IBDMDB, IIBDGC, Open Targets, FinnGen, and UK Biobank
Primary publications and supplementary tables linked from repository records
Dataset-specific README files, sample metadata tables, and controlled-access study descriptions

The initial entries should not be promoted to Verified until a human curator has checked the repository record and primary publication.

This registry is a curated research resource and is provided for dataset discovery purposes only. It does not constitute clinical guidance, a systematic review, or a quality certification of any dataset. All metadata should be independently verified before use in any analysis.

title: About
text:
The IBD Omics Explorer is a curated academic tool designed to support the discovery and navigation of publicly available omics datasets in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) research. It is maintained as part of ongoing research at Stanford University School of Medicine.


IBD omics data are distributed across many repositories, portals, and publications, making comprehensive dataset discovery time-consuming. This explorer aims to aggregate metadata from publicly accessible resources into a single searchable and filterable registry, helping researchers:

Identify datasets relevant to a given research question
Understand the availability and access conditions of public IBD data
Compare available datasets across omics layers, tissue types, and disease groups
Plan analyses that leverage multiple complementary datasets

This is a dataset discovery tool. It does not rank, recommend, or certify datasets. All metadata should be verified before analysis.





It is not a systematic review or meta-analysis of IBD omics data.
It does not assess dataset quality or analytical suitability.
It does not rank or recommend any dataset as better or worse for any purpose.
It is not a complete or authoritative registry of all IBD omics data.
It does not provide access to data files directly; it links to source repositories.





The registry currently covers:

Bulk transcriptomics (microarray and RNA-seq)
Single-cell transcriptomics
Spatial transcriptomics
Microbiome (metagenomics, metatranscriptomics)
Proteomics and metaproteomics
Metabolomics and lipidomics
Methylation and epigenomics
Genetics and GWAS
Multi-omics resources

See the Omics Layers page for a layer-by-layer inventory, and the Curation Methods page for detailed inclusion criteria and metadata definitions.




The dataset registry is maintained in data/ibd_omics_datasets.csv. Entries carry verification fields indicating whether source records, publication metadata, access status, and sample metadata have been checked. Entries marked pending_review should be confirmed against source repositories before use.
The registry is designed to grow. If you identify datasets that should be included, metadata errors, or outdated access information, contributions are welcome.




Dahham Alsoud Postdoctoral Researcher Stanford University School of Medicine GitHub | Google Scholar


This website is a curated research tool provided for academic dataset discovery. It is not a clinical resource. Dataset availability, access conditions, and metadata should be verified independently before any analysis. The registry does not constitute an endorsement of any dataset, repository, or research group.

title: Dataset Explorer
text: This table lists primary accession-level datasets currently indexed in the IBD Omics Explorer. Use the search box and filters to narrow results. All metadata are conservative annotations derived from public records; fields marked Unclear require manual verification. Integrated atlases, portals, databases, and consortium resources are tracked on the Integrated Resources page.

title: Dataset Explorer
text: Filters and Search

title: Dataset Explorer
text:
Data Access Status Legend







Label
Meaning




Public raw data
Raw data files (FASTQ, CEL, IDAT, mzML, count matrices, etc.) are publicly downloadable


Public processed data
Processed expression/abundance matrix is available; raw data may not be public


Public portal
Data are accessible via a public portal or database


Controlled access
Indexed publicly but requires application or institutional approval


Publication only
Described in a publication; reusable data files are not clearly available


Unclear
Repository record exists but data availability or metadata are incomplete




Note. Data access status and metadata were assigned conservatively based on public records available at the time of curation. Availability may change. Always verify directly with the source repository before initiating analysis.

title: Integrated Resources
text: This page separates integrated resources from primary datasets. These resources are useful for discovery, annotation, meta-analysis, and target prioritization, but they are not single source datasets with one accession, one sample count, or one uniform metadata model.

title: Integrated Resources
text: Resource Table

title: Integrated Resources
text:
Source Dataset Coverage

This audit tracks source accessions named by integrated resources and whether each one is already represented as a primary row in the Dataset Explorer. Direct IBD cohorts are marked as add candidates; atlas, fetal, reference, controlled-access, or unclear components are retained here for review instead of being forced into the primary dataset table.

title: Integrated Resources
text:
How to Interpret This Page
Integrated resources may combine many source datasets, reprocess data with their own pipelines, or expose only derived summaries. They should not replace the original accession-level records when you need raw data, exact sample metadata, or reproducible cohort definitions.
Use this page to find:

IBD-specific single-cell atlases such as scIBD
broader gut atlases that include IBD, such as the Gut Cell Atlas
curated databases such as IBDTransDB
consortium and target-prioritization resources such as IIBDGC, GWAS Catalog, and Open Targets

When a resource points to specific source accessions, those primary datasets should remain in the Dataset Explorer.

title: Orchestrator
text: The orchestrator page turns the explorer into an active curation system. It tracks missing datasets, ambiguous resource components, verification gaps, and the next concrete tasks needed to make the registry more complete and useful.

title: Orchestrator
text: Improvement Queue

title: Orchestrator
text:
Operating Method
The orchestrator uses a conservative loop:

Search for missing resources and component accessions.
Separate primary datasets from integrated portals, atlases, and consortium resources.
Verify source metadata before adding a row to the primary Dataset Explorer.
Keep uncertain components visible with manual-review status instead of forcing a category.
Promote only source-backed rows into the primary table.
Re-render the explorer and check that users can find the new information.

This method is designed to move the field forward by making hidden dataset provenance visible, while avoiding inflated counts or unsupported clinical assumptions.

title: Use Cases
text: This page organizes datasets by potential research use case based on available metadata tags. This page does not recommend, rank, or endorse any dataset. Entries listed under each use case are tagged as potentially relevant based on publicly available metadata. Suitability for any specific analysis must be assessed by the researcher based on study design, sample size, platform, access status, and metadata completeness.

title: Use Cases
text:
Adult vs. Pediatric Comparisons


These datasets are tagged as potentially relevant to this use case based on available metadata. Pediatric cohorts are specifically labeled where known; adult designation reflects the majority of unlabeled cohorts but should be verified.

title: Use Cases
text:
Mucosal Disease-vs-Control Transcriptomics


Datasets with intestinal mucosal biopsies from IBD and non-IBD controls, potentially useful for disease-vs-control transcriptomic analyses. Tissue site (ileum vs. colon vs. rectum) and platform should be verified before pooling studies.

title: Use Cases
text:
Blood-Based Transcriptomics


Datasets using whole blood or peripheral blood as the source tissue. Blood-based transcriptomics is sensitive to cell composition variability and should be interpreted with attention to disease activity, treatment status, and cell-type deconvolution.

title: Use Cases
text:
CD vs. UC Comparison


Datasets including both Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis samples, potentially relevant for direct CD-vs-UC comparisons. Tissue site matching between CD and UC samples is important for valid comparisons.

title: Use Cases
text:
Disease Activity and Longitudinal Analysis


Datasets with disease activity information or longitudinal follow-up, potentially relevant for studying dynamic changes, remission, or treatment response over time. Longitudinal datasets are particularly valuable but also more complex to analyze.

title: Use Cases
text:
Cell-Type Reference and Deconvolution


Single-cell and reference datasets potentially useful for building or applying cell-type deconvolution models to bulk transcriptomic data. Cell annotation quality and tissue site matching to the bulk dataset should be considered carefully.

title: Use Cases
text:
Spatial Tissue Organization


Datasets using spatial transcriptomics to characterize the spatial organization of cell types and gene expression within IBD tissue sections. Platform-specific resolution and gene panel coverage should be considered.

title: Use Cases
text:
Treatment Response Analysis


Datasets with treatment metadata or specifically designed to study treatment response. These are potentially relevant for analyzing biological correlates of response or non-response to IBD therapies. Treatment status at biopsy and response definitions vary across studies.

title: Use Cases
text:
Host–Microbiome Integration


Datasets including microbiome or multi-omics data relevant to host–microbiome research in IBD. Integration of host and microbial data requires careful sample-level matching and statistical methods accounting for compositionality and sparsity.

title: Use Cases
text:
Fecal Proteomics and Metaproteomics


Datasets using stool-derived proteomics or metaproteomics, capturing both host-derived and microbial proteins non-invasively. Fecal proteomics is sensitive to sample handling and storage conditions.

title: Use Cases
text:
Metabolomics and Lipidomics


Datasets measuring small molecules or lipids in IBD-relevant samples. Platform and sample matrix (stool, blood, urine, tissue) substantially affect what is measured and how results should be interpreted.

title: Use Cases
text:
Methylation and Epigenomics


Datasets measuring DNA methylation or chromatin state in IBD-relevant tissues. Cell composition confounding in blood samples and probe coverage limitations in older arrays (450K) are important considerations.

title: Use Cases
text:
Genetics and Target Prioritization


Genetic resources including GWAS summary statistics and individual-level genotype data. Useful for genetic risk factor mapping, colocalization, Mendelian randomization, and target prioritization. Individual-level data are largely controlled-access.







Note. Use-case groupings are based on metadata tags in the registry CSV and are designed to assist in dataset discovery only. They do not constitute a recommendation that any listed dataset is suitable for the stated use case. Researchers should independently verify data quality, study design, sample characteristics, and access conditions before initiating any analysis.
